_meta.title: Join Algorithms — Nested Loop, Hash, Sort-Merge

questions.question: Which join algorithm is optimal when one relation is very small and the other has an index on the join key?
options: Grace Hash Join
Index Nested Loop Join
Sort-Merge Join
Block Nested Loop Join
questions.explanation: Index NLJ uses the small relation as the outer loop and probes the index on the inner relation for each outer tuple, resulting in O(|R| · log|S|) comparisons rather than O(|R| · |S|).

questions.question: Hash join can only be used for which type of join predicate?
options: Range predicates (R.a < S.b)
Inequality predicates (R.a != S.b)
Equality predicates (R.a = S.b)
Any predicate type
questions.explanation: Hash join relies on hashing the join key to the same bucket, which only works with equality predicates. Range and inequality joins require NLJ or Sort-Merge.

questions.question: Grace Hash Join handles large relations by:
options: Sorting both relations and merging them in lockstep
Using a B-tree index to look up matching tuples
Partitioning both relations into N buckets on disk, then joining each partition individually
Increasing work_mem to fit the entire relation in memory
questions.explanation: Grace Hash Join has two phases: (1) partition both relations using a hash function, writing partitions to disk; (2) for each partition, load the build side into memory, build a hash table, and probe with the other side. This handles any-size relations with limited memory.

questions.question: A Sort-Merge Join has an I/O cost advantage over Hash Join when:
options: The join predicate is an equality
One or both inputs are already sorted on the join key
The relations fit entirely in memory
There is no index available
questions.explanation: If an input is already sorted (from an ORDER BY clause or sorted index scan), Sort-Merge skips the sort phase for that input, reducing I/O. Hash Join still requires a full pass to build the hash table regardless of input order.

questions.question: Hybrid Hash Join improves on Grace Hash Join by:
options: Using a better hash function to reduce collisions
Keeping the first partition in memory during the partitioning phase, saving 2 page I/Os for that partition
Sorting the input before hashing to improve cache locality
Using multiple hash functions in parallel
questions.explanation: Hybrid Hash Join keeps partition 0's tuples in memory during partitioning, avoiding the write-to-disk and read-back I/O for the first partition. This reduces total I/O from 3·(P_R + P_S) to approximately 3·(P_R + P_S) - 2·(P_R + P_S)/fanout.

questions.question: PostgreSQL's join planner will choose Sort-Merge Join over Hash Join when:
options: work_mem is very large
The join predicate is a non-equi condition (e.g., R.a < S.b)
The outer relation has fewer tuples than the inner relation
Both relations have no indexes
questions.explanation: Sort-Merge Join handles both equi-joins and non-equi joins (range, inequality). Hash Join only supports equality predicates. When the predicate is non-equi, the planner eliminates Hash Join and chooses between NLJ and SMJ.
